back: A hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes, where each node contains data and references to child nodes, facilitating efficient data organization and retrieval.
A fundamental part of a tree that stores data and contains references to other nodes (children). Each node can represent a data point or a position in the structure.
The connection between two nodes in a tree, representing the relationship or link from a parent node to a child node.
The topmost node in a tree, from which all other nodes descend. It does not have a parent node.
A sequence of nodes and edges connecting a node with a descendant, showing the route from one node to another within the tree.
Nodes that are directly connected and descend from another node (the parent) within a tree.
A node that has one or more nodes (children) directly under it in the hierarchy.
Nodes that share the same parent and reside at the same level within a tree.
A portion of a tree structure consisting of a node and all its descendants, forming a tree itself.
A node with no children, representing an endpoint of a tree.
The layer or depth of a node, starting at zero for the root and increasing by one for each step down.
The total number of levels in a tree, or the length of the longest path from the root to a leaf node.
A type of tree where each node has at most two children, typically called the left and right children.
A method for implementing a tree using a nested list structure where each list represents a node and its children.
A tree implementation method where each node contains its data and explicit links to its child nodes.
A tree representing the syntactic structure of a string according to a formal grammar, used in compilers.
A binary tree used to represent expressions where internal nodes are operators and leaf nodes are operands.
A tree traversal where each node is processed before its child nodes (root, left, right).
A tree traversal where each node is processed after its child nodes (left, right, root).
A traversal for binary trees where nodes are processed in the order: left-child, root, right-child.
An abstract data type where each element has a priority, and elements are served based on that priority.
A binary tree-based structure that satisfies the heap property, where nodes are ordered relative to their children.
A binary tree where every level except possibly the last is filled, and all nodes are as far left as possible.
A requirement that a heap must be a complete binary tree to ensure balance.
A requirement maintaining a specific order between a node and its children (max-heap or min-heap).
A heap where the value of each node is greater than or equal to its children, keeping the maximum value at the root.
A heap where the value of each node is less than or equal to its children, keeping the minimum value at the root.
A binary tree where each node's key is greater than all keys in its left subtree and less than those in its right subtree.
The defining rule for a BST: left subtree elements are less than the node, and right subtree elements are greater.
The node that immediately follows a given node in an inorder traversal of a binary search tree.
A function that returns an iterator and yields values one at a time, pausing between each execution.
A BST that automatically adjusts to maintain a low height, ensuring minimal path lengths.
In AVL trees, the difference between the heights of a node's left and right subtrees.
A self-balancing BST where the height of subtrees differs by at most one, ensuring logarithmic time complexity.
